concept: Free money for university exists — but it goes to the students who find it, apply early, and stack multiple sources together.

hook.fact: Two students. Identical qualifications. One pays full price. One pays 40% less.
hook.question: The difference? One applied for scholarships. The other assumed they would not qualify.

conceptCards.title: Card 1
conceptCards.body: **Scholarships** are awarded based on merit — academic performance, talent, leadership, or community contribution. They do not need to be repaid. A student who does not apply does not receive one.

conceptCards.title: Card 2
conceptCards.body: **Grants** and **financial aid** are awarded based on financial need. Universities, governments, and NGOs all offer them. US universities in particular use need-based aid to bring their headline price down significantly for eligible families.

conceptCards.title: Card 3
conceptCards.body: Most scholarships have application deadlines 6–12 months before your programme starts. Applying late is the single most common reason qualified students miss funding. Build a scholarship calendar before you apply to universities.

conceptCards.title: Card 4
conceptCards.body: Stacking means applying for multiple small scholarships simultaneously. Three awards of $2,000 each are worth the same as one $6,000 scholarship — and the smaller ones attract fewer applicants. Most students stop at one application. The students who fund their

conceptCards.title: Card 5
conceptCards.body: External scholarships — from companies, foundations, charities, and governments — exist separately from university aid. They are less well-known, which makes them less competitive. Your own government may offer study-abroad scholarships you have never heard of. Research your home country's

conceptCards.title: Card 6
conceptCards.body: Now test this: For US universities, the **FAFSA** (Free Application for Federal Student Aid) is the gateway to most need-based aid. International students are ineligible for FAFSA, but many US universities have their own institutional aid for international students —

questions.prompt: What is the key difference between a scholarship and a grant?
options: Scholarships are merit-based; grants are need-based
Grants do not need to be repaid but scholarships do
Scholarships are only for domestic students
questions.explanation: "Scholarships reward achievement — grades, talent, or contribution. Grants and financial aid respond to financial circumstances."

questions.statement: International students cannot receive any financial aid at US universities.
questions.whyPrompt: Why is that true or false?
questions.explanation: "While international students cannot access FAFSA federal aid, many US universities offer institutional aid packages specifically for international students — eligibility varies and must be checked per institution."

questions.prompt: Match each funding type to its source:

pairs.right: Awarded for academic or extracurricular achievement
Awarded based on family financial circumstances
Provided by your home country for overseas study
Offered by the university itself, separate from FAFSA

questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.sentence: "Applying for multiple scholarships simultaneously to combine smaller awards into a significant total is called ___."
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.prompt: Put these scholarship application steps in the correct order:
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.prompt: Sofia applies for 8 scholarships averaging $1,800 each, with a 30% success rate per application.
questions.explanation: "8 × 30% = 2.4 expected scholarships. At $1,800 each, that is approximately $4,320 in expected funding — a meaningful contribution to her annual gap."

questions.sentence: Complete the sentence: "A smaller scholarship with ___ applicants often has a better expected return than a larger scholarship with ___ applicants, because the ___ of winning is higher."
questions.explanation: Review the concept and try again.

questions.scenario: Kai found a £5,000 scholarship for UK engineering students. He plans to apply in July — three months before his September start date.
options: The scholarship is too small to be worth applying for
Kai missed the deadline by approximately 6 months
UK scholarships are only for domestic students
questions.explanation: "Most scholarships close 6–12 months before a programme starts. July is far too late for a September intake. Kai needed to be researching scholarships in October–November of the previous year."

tasks.scenario: Marcus needs to reduce his €8,000 annual funding gap. Build his scholarship strategy:
tasks.explanation: "With a 20–30% success rate per application, Marcus needs 10+ applications to reliably close his funding gap. Three strong applications may yield nothing. Ten gives him statistical coverage."

tasks.prompt: Which gives her the better expected return?
tasks.explanation: "Scholarship A: €10,000 × (1/500) ≈ €20 expected value. Scholarship B: €2,500 × (1/40) ≈ €62 expected value. Scholarship B returns 3× more per application — better odds are often worth more than bigger prizes."

lockIn.takeaway: Scholarships go to students who apply early, apply often, and look beyond the obvious — not just those with the

reviewQueueEntry.definition: Merit vs need-based funding anchors better education money decisions.
reviewQueueEntry.quickRecallPrompt: What does Merit vs need-based funding include beyond tuition?
